summary: The notebook demonstrates a brute-force combinatorial search to find the optimal word permutation for the first sample in the Santa 2024 competition by minimizing perplexity. It implements a custom batched perplexity scorer using a 4-bit quantized Gemma-2-9B model to accelerate GPU metric evaluation compared to standard single-sample processing. By iterating through all 3.6 million possible permutations and processing them in batches of 64, the author efficiently identifies a permutation that achieves a perplexity score near 470. The key takeaway is that leveraging large batch sizes with quantized LLMs dramatically speeds up metric computation, making exhaustive searches feasible on consumer-grade GPUs. Readers learn how to adapt official metric code for batch processing and apply it to discrete optimization tasks.
competition_flow: Reads the first sample's text from the submission file, generates all possible word permutations, evaluates their perplexity using a batched quantized Gemma-2-9B model, and outputs the optimal permutation merged into a new submission CSV.
data_reading: Reads sample_submission.csv using pandas.read_csv to extract the original text for the first sample.
data_processing: Splits the original text into words, generates all possible permutations using itertools.permutations, and joins them back into strings.
Normalizes whitespace by splitting and rejoining strings before passing them to the scorer.
insights: Using large batch sizes with quantized LLMs significantly accelerates metric computation on GPUs compared to processing samples individually.
Exhaustive brute-force search over all permutations is computationally feasible for small combinatorial spaces when metric evaluation is optimized.
Correctly shifting logits and labels is required to compute token-level cross-entropy loss for accurate perplexity calculation.
